params.text:
Head and spinal injuries

A blow to the head can range from a mild bump to a serious brain injury. Watch for warning signs that develop after a knock to the head: drowsiness, confusion, repeated vomiting, a severe headache, unequal pupils, or fluid or blood coming from the nose or ears. These are red flags — call 999.

If someone has fallen from a height, had a heavy fall, or has neck or back pain after an accident, suspect a spinal injury. Unnecessary movement could cause lasting harm, so encourage them to keep still and gently support their head in the position you found it while you wait for help.

params.alt: Illustration for Head, spine and bones

params.text:
Broken bones, sprains and strains

You cannot always tell a fracture from a sprain, so if you are in any doubt, treat it as the more serious injury. Signs of a broken bone include pain, swelling, bruising, an odd shape, and being unable to use the limb.

  • Keep it still: support the injured part in the position that is most comfortable and avoid moving it unnecessarily.
  • Do not move the person needlessly: only move someone who is in danger, and never force a limb straight.
  • Cool a sprain: a cold pack wrapped in a cloth can ease pain and swelling in a soft-tissue injury.
  • Get the right help: call 999 for an open or severe fracture, and arrange other medical care for lesser injuries.

params.question: If someone may have a spinal injury after a fall, you should quickly move them into a more comfortable position to help them relax.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. This is false: moving someone with a suspected spinal injury can cause lasting harm, so keep them still and support the head until help arrives.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this is false. Unnecessary movement can worsen a spinal injury, so keep the person still, support their head in the position found, and wait for the ambulance.
